description: Planner-selected human debugging experience cards for Genesis simulation generation.

cards.title: Inspect assets when failures may come from generated geometry or mechanism affordances
cards.summary: Planner should choose inspect_assets when execution evidence cannot distinguish body/action bugs from generated mesh/XML defects, then route metadata updates, regeneration, or local repair based on inspection evidence.
guidance: Use inspect_assets when logs/video/metrics suggest a generated asset may be the cause but the evidence does not yet prove whether the problem is intrinsic asset quality or local placement/control.

Inspect generated meshes when failures involve wrong scale, impossible clearance, unexpected collision shape, missing texture, bad orientation, failed FEM import, or topology that cannot provide the requested contact.
Inspect XML/MJCF assets when failures involve missing actuator affordances, wrong joint axes, impossible grasp cages, invalid link hierarchy, or morphology that cannot perform the task.
If inspection shows geometry is acceptable but scale/bbox metadata is wrong, use update_mesh_asset_metadata.
If inspection shows intrinsic mesh/XML defects, revise the affected asset request and use start_mesh_assets or start_xml_assets.
If inspection shows assets are usable, route repair to scene/body/action/rendering according to the source-level evidence.
restrictions: Do not repeatedly ask body/action to repair around an asset whose morphology or topology cannot perform the task.
Do not regenerate assets for metadata-only sizing mistakes when update_mesh_asset_metadata is sufficient.
checks: Check asset_manifest entries, validation reports, preview images, contact sheets, bbox/scale metadata, and generated XML source before choosing the repair owner.
Check whether the failure persists after plausible placement/clearance fixes before declaring an asset intrinsically unsuitable.
dispatch_hints: Send to Planner after asset-related execution or critic failures.
Send to Critic when recommending planner-owned asset inspection or regeneration.

provenance.motivation: Make inspect_assets a first-class routing option for ambiguous generated-asset failures.

cards.title: Generated cases must not depend on packaged Genesis assets
cards.summary: Task geometry, robots, textures, and props should come from primitives, case-workspace generated assets, or user-provided layout assets, not from genesis/assets.
restrictions: Do not inspect, copy, import, or reference prepackaged assets under genesis/assets.
Do not use gs.utils.get_assets_dir or Genesis built-in relative asset paths for generated task geometry.
XML/MJCF mesh references are allowed only when mesh files were generated into the same case workspace and validated by the XML asset pipeline.
checks: Run static validation for planner outputs and generated source when asset paths appear.
Treat forbidden built-in asset references as source-level failures, not harmless shortcuts.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when asset requests or XML prompts are being written.
Send to body/rendering when generated code instantiates meshes, robots, textures, or props.

cards.title: Evaluate generated assets before blaming only action timing
cards.summary: When a task depends on generated mesh or XML morphology, inspect shape, topology, joints, actuator affordances, scale, and orientation before assigning a local body/action repair.
guidance: Treat generated assets as first-class evidence, not as trusted black boxes. Inspect asset manifests, generated XML/MJCF source, worker reports, preview images, and in-scene contact sheets when the task depends on generated assets.
Judge whether the asset's shape, topology, joint axes, actuator contract, scale, and visible geometry can plausibly perform the requested task before blaming only action timing.

For grasping/manipulation, check whether fingers and thumb can form a real opposing cage, whether the payload fits inside that grasp volume, and whether closing the actuators would trap rather than sweep the payload away.
Set recommended_owner to planner when asset morphology, topology, joints, actuators, scale, orientation, or visible geometry cannot plausibly perform the task.
If the generated asset is plausible but scene placement, initial clearance, material/coupling, or code-side metadata is wrong, route to body/scene.
If the asset and placement are plausible but timing, controller, gates, or release sequence are wrong, route to action.
dispatch_hints: Send to critic whenever mesh/XML assets are central to the requested behavior.
Send to planner when deciding whether to inspect assets or regenerate asset prompts.

cards.title: Generated assets must match the requested shape before source-level tuning
cards.summary: Planner should verify generated mesh/XML assets for semantic shape, scale, aspect ratio, thickness, texture placement, and complexity before asking body or action workers to tune around them.
guidance: Use procedural primitives or simple CAD-like geometry for plain boxes, cuboids, cylinders, rods, plates, pads, blocks, cups, gauges, and other simple shapes instead of generating decorative meshes.
For generated mesh assets, inspect preview images, manifest bbox, aspect ratios, orientation, thickness, semantic silhouette, texture visibility, and mesh complexity before body/action tuning.
For FEM assets, reject assets that pass manifold or FEM import checks but are too thin, too small, overcomplex, spiky, high-element-count, or semantically unlike the requested object.
For XML/MJCF mechanisms, inspect whether the generated morphology exposes the required joints, actuators, collision affordances, grasp surfaces, and contact geometry.

If the asset's intrinsic shape is wrong, revise the Planner asset request and regenerate; if only scale metadata is wrong and the shape is otherwise correct, use metadata update rather than source-level workarounds.
restrictions: Do not ask body.py to procedurally reshape, non-uniformly scale, or hide an intrinsically wrong generated mesh.
Do not accept an asset just because manifold validation, FEM import, or XML parsing passed; semantic shape and task affordance must also match.
Do not use thin spikes, hair-like details, decorative protrusions, or high-complexity surfaces for tasks that need robust FEM contact or simple rigid collision.
checks: Check requested dimensions against asset_manifest bbox and final runtime scale.
Check preview/contact-sheet appearance for recognizable semantic shape and requested color or texture markers.
Check mesh vertex/face/tet counts and whether complexity is appropriate for the task and runtime budget.
Check whether the failure should route to Planner asset regeneration, metadata update, or local body/action repair.
dispatch_hints: Send to Planner before start_mesh_assets or start_xml_assets when a task depends on generated morphology.
Send to Critic when an execution runs but the object shape, thickness, texture, or mechanism affordance does not match the prompt.
Send to Body/Rendering when using a ready asset so they verify bbox, scale, visual mesh, and runtime mesh before integration.

provenance.motivation: Workspace failures showed generated assets that were valid files but wrong in proportion, thickness, semantic identity, texture visibility, or complexity.

cards.title: Generated mesh workers should use the asset manifest, not guessed paths or ad hoc replacements
cards.summary: Generated mesh and procedural cloth mesh assets must be instantiated from ready asset_manifest entries with their runtime mesh, scale, coordinate metadata, texture metadata, and simulation role preserved.
guidance: For generated mesh assets, read assets/asset_manifest.json and use entries with source_type == generated_mesh and status == ready.
For FEM cloth assets, read entries with source_type == cloth_mesh and status == ready; these are surface meshes for gs.materials.FEM.Cloth and are not required to be watertight.

For procedural FEM cloth assets, inspect validation.cloth_mesh.target_edge_length, median_edge_length, and face_count to verify the Planner's requested cloth_target_edge_length produced an appropriate resolution.
Instantiate runtime geometry from entry.runtime_path using the manifest scale, file_meshes_are_zup metadata, visual_path, and intended fixed/dynamic role.
runtime_path is the strict-manifold simulation/collision mesh.
visual_path is a seam-aware textured render mesh attached to the same rigid entity, not a separate object to instantiate as an independent simulation body.
texture_path is evidence metadata for the transferred base-color image and texture preview checks.
Use the manifest's logical names, scale factors, coordinate metadata, texture paths, and simulation roles instead of guessing mesh paths, sizes, or orientation.

If a generated mesh manifest entry is missing, failed, invalid, or cannot be imported, fail clearly and report that Planner should regenerate the mesh asset; do not edit mesh files or approximate the object with ad hoc primitives.
dispatch_hints: Send to body/rendering when generated mesh assets are being instantiated.
Send to critic when mesh scale, texture, orientation, or manifest consistency is part of the evidence.
Send to planner when deciding whether to update mesh metadata or regenerate an asset.

cards.title: Planner asset retries should rewrite the owning asset request, not patch around bad assets
cards.summary: Planner should distinguish intrinsic generated-asset defects from metadata-only issues and route retries through the correct mesh/XML asset action with a complete revised planner_output.
guidance: Meshy mesh generation accepts at most 800 characters in the final mesh-agent prompt; keep each generated_mesh request concise enough for the assembled prompt.
When mesh feedback requires a different prompt, rewrite the affected asset request into a concise integrated description instead of appending repair text.
Put the revised complete planner_output directly on the start_mesh_assets action that retries generation; action rationale, notes, and repair_brief are not sent to the mesh agent.
Mesh asset generation owns mesh validity. If manifold validation, texture transfer, Genesis FEM import validation, or generated topology fails, regenerate through the mesh asset action.
Do not ask body/scene/action/rendering workers to rewrite, procedurally repair, reshape, retopologize, or approximate mesh-intrinsic defects.
If a ready generated mesh has acceptable geometry but wrong runtime scale/bbox metadata, use update_mesh_asset_metadata with a complete revised planner_output rather than retrying mesh generation.

For XML/MJCF asset feedback, rewrite the affected XML asset request in a revised complete planner_output and put it directly on start_xml_assets; do not rely on rationale, notes, or repair_brief to reach the XML asset worker.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when generated mesh/XML asset reports or inspections show defects.
Send to critic when deciding whether a failure belongs to Planner-owned asset regeneration or local body/action repair.

cards.title: Use generated mesh assets for textured or complex static objects
cards.summary: When requested appearance depends on texture, pattern, ornamentation, or complex non-articulated object structure, Planner should prefer Meshy-generated mesh assets over primitive approximations.
guidance: Any object whose requested appearance depends on texture, patterned surface detail, decorative material variation, image-like surface content, or nontrivial visual ornamentation should be represented by a Meshy-generated asset request, even when the task does not explicitly say mesh.

If an object has a complex recognizable shape, organic silhouette, decorative pattern, or texture, and it does not need joints, actuators, or articulation, prefer a Meshy generated_mesh asset instead of approximating it with many primitives or plain colors.
Do not ask writers to fake texture-dependent objects with plain primitive colors or simple Genesis surfaces.
When visual appearance matters, include texture needs and simulation role in the asset request so downstream workers can use manifest texture metadata and critic can verify rendered texture evidence.
restrictions: Do not use Meshy for objects that need controllable joints, hinges, sliders, or articulated mechanisms; route those to XML/MJCF asset requests instead.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when writing planner_output asset_requests.
Send to critic/rendering when visual texture evidence is central to task success.

cards.title: XML/MJCF asset requests should describe mechanism affordances and preserve actuator contracts
cards.summary: Planner should request XML/MJCF assets for articulated mechanisms with explicit joints, actuators, base behavior, dimensions, and downstream body/action contracts.
guidance: For articulated robots, grippers, gates, latches, actuated mechanisms, or any task object best represented as one self-contained MJCF body tree with joints and actuators, add an XML/MJCF asset request with asset_type generated_xml or mjcf.
Describe the required joints, actuator semantics, base behavior, approximate dimensions, and control affordances in purpose/simulation_role.
XML/MJCF assets may use primitive geoms or generated case-workspace mesh files, but must never reference Genesis built-in meshes, external mesh downloads, hfields, or texture files.
Do not use XML/MJCF only for textured decorative mesh objects.

When the plan uses XML/MJCF actuators, make body/action contracts explicit: body must expose stable actuator names, joint names, DOF groups, or control handles in actors, and action must drive those handles with Genesis actuator/DOF/force control APIs after initialization.
For fixed-base IPC/external_articulation mechanisms, explicitly distinguish active task-contact geoms from bookkeeping mount/base geoms in the XML asset request.

If a dummy mount/base geom is needed so IPC treats the articulation as well-formed, request it as tiny, named, reported, and far outside the active interaction volume with positive initial clearance from all task objects. It should use the smallest practical nonzero size that satisfies importer/IPC bookkeeping, and it should not sit in a roller gap, gripper cage, containment opening, slot, chute, support path, object trajectory, or exit path.

For roller, spool, paddle, press, or gripper mechanisms, do not allow a single mount at one end of the active contact span. Put mounts outside the swept/contact region, make them symmetric if they are physical, or make them remote bookkeeping-only geoms that cannot affect task contact.

For deformable squeeze or transport mechanisms, assume the soft body can bulge sideways and along the roller/tool axis; mount/base geoms must be outside that expanded bbox and must have positive initial clearance.
Do not ask action to create motion for an XML articulated asset by overwriting root pose, qpos, or velocities during simulation.
If the actuator contract is missing, generated code should fail clearly so critic can assign a source-aware body/action repair.
checks: Inspect generated XML for mount/base geoms near active contact paths; route to XML asset regeneration if they can collide with, guide, block, wedge, or deflect task objects.
Check that active tool surfaces keep collision enabled while dummy mount/base geoms are far from task objects and cannot become hidden obstacles.
Check that XML asset reports identify dummy mounts separately from active contact geoms and state their pose, minimal nonzero size, initial-clearance margin, and safety rationale.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when creating or rewriting XML/MJCF asset requests.
Send to body/action when generated XML actuators need stable runtime handles and physical control paths.
Send to critic when mechanism failure may be caused by underspecified asset affordances.

cards.title: Independent task objects should keep collision enabled by default
cards.summary: Independent physical objects should default to mutually enabled collision/contact; avoid pair, mask, or contact-filter overrides except for narrowly scoped self-collision inside the same articulated body.
restrictions: Do not rely on visual-only geometry for task-critical physical participants.

Default to enabling collision/contact for every pair of independent physical task objects, including passive objects, supports, obstacles, containers, targets, robot/tool contact surfaces, ground/table surfaces, and generated asset collision geoms.

Do not override collision pairs, collision groups, masks, contype/conaffinity, exclude lists, or per-body filters to disable contact between independent objects unless the exception is explicit, localized, and physically justified.

The routine exception is internal self-collision filtering within the same articulated body, when needed to avoid adjacent-link contact, actuator self-locking, or dummy mount collisions. This exception must not disable collision between robot/tool contact surfaces and task objects.

A second narrow exception is non-task dummy mount/base geometry created only for fixed-base IPC/external_articulation bookkeeping. Such mount geometry must be far outside the main simulation/contact region, while active links, tools, rollers, fingers, paddles, supports, and containers must remain collidable.

Dummy mount/base geometry must not be placed where it can physically affect the requested interaction: not in a roller gap, gripper cage, containment opening, slot, chute, support path, object trajectory, or exit path.

For deformable squeeze/transport cases, treat reachable end mounts, central mounts, and one-sided base blocks as task-interfering hidden obstacles unless they are outside the maximum expected soft-body sweep and initially separated by positive clearance.

Do not set task objects, supports, targets, gripper fingers, trays, paddles, cups, platforms, or other task-contact geoms to contype/conaffinity settings that make them ghost through ordinary objects.
Do not rely on objects passing through each other, one-sided visual shells, hidden supports, or disabled collision masks to make the result look plausible.
Avoid initial interpenetration; use positive clearances and metrics/logs that can reveal penetration or support loss.
If a collision override is genuinely necessary, document the exact excluded pair, the reason, and why it does not remove any task-relevant contact path.
checks: Verify task-critical objects have collision geometry rather than only surfaces, textures, sites, or visual shells.
Inspect source code and generated XML/MJCF for collision masks, contype/conaffinity settings, contact exclusions, collision groups, and pair-filter overrides that disable inter-object contact.
Confirm that any disabled pair is internal to one articulated body or otherwise explicitly documented as non-task-relevant.

When inspecting XML/MJCF, separately classify bookkeeping mount/base geoms versus active task-contact geoms. Mount geoms near the interaction path with ordinary contact settings are hidden obstacles, not useful physical participants.
Check initial clearances to dummy mount/base geoms explicitly; do not accept a plan where dummy mounts are close enough to overlap, guide, block, wedge, or deflect deformables or manipulated objects.
For paired rollers, presses, or grippers, check whether a soft body or passive object can laterally bulge into a mount even if initial clearance is positive.
Check the event log or metrics for contact counts, penetration indicators, or support loss when relevant.
dispatch_hints: Send to scene/body for collision geometry, masks, and initial clearance.
Send to critic when a video looks plausible but contact evidence is missing or ghosted.

cards.title: External-articulation XML needs fixed-base joints and real collision geoms
cards.summary: MJCF/URDF assets coupled through external_articulation should expose fixed-base articulated DOFs, collision geometry on participating links, and actuator-driven motion rather than root teleports.
guidance: Use fixed-base articulations with revolute or prismatic child bodies for driven mechanisms.
Give every IPC-participating link real nonzero collision geometry, including tiny dummy collision geometry on logical mount parents when needed.

A dummy parent geom may be a primitive MJCF geom such as type="box", but it must be nonzero-volume collision geometry rather than a site, inertial marker, visual-only marker, zero-area plane, line, or empty body.
Use dummy mount geometry only as IPC/articulation bookkeeping, not as a physical obstacle, guide, stop, wedge, support, ramp, or collision feature in the requested task.

When the XML or IPC path needs a nonzero dummy collision geom on a fixed-base parent, make that geom as small as practical while still satisfying the importer/IPC bookkeeping requirement, and place it far outside the main simulation/contact region with positive initial clearance from every task object. The dummy mount should be so small and remote that it cannot affect the scene even if ordinary collision handling includes it.

Never put a dummy mount inside a task passage, roller gap, gripper cage, containment opening, slot, chute, support path, object trajectory, or exit path. It must not become a hidden stop, wedge, ramp, wall, or unilateral side obstacle.

For paired mechanisms such as rollers, spools, presses, or paddles, avoid a single mount at one end of the active span. For deformable squeeze/transport tasks, assume the soft body may bulge sideways or along the roller axis; dummy mounts must be far beyond the maximum possible soft-body bbox and outside the swept/contact volume.
Keep active child/driven contact surfaces collision-enabled with ordinary contact settings, but keep bookkeeping mount/base geoms far away from all task objects and interaction paths.

When coup_collision_links is used as a positive IPC collision filter, account for fixed-joint merge behavior: collision geometry authored on fixed child links may be merged into a parent/target link such as rotor_link. Include the merge target link in coup_collision_links in addition to the semantic child contact links, otherwise the visible clamp/finger/socket may move while its merged IPC collision geometry is filtered out.
For multi-part clamps or sockets built from fixed child bodies, report both the semantic active link names and any fixed-merge target links that must remain collision-enabled.
Name and report dummy mount/base geoms explicitly, including role, position, size, why the size is minimal, and why they are far enough away to not affect task motion.
Child/driven geoms such as spools, hinges, paddles, presses, and gripper fingers must have real collision geometry; do not provide only visual geometry for IPC contact participants.
Set joint axes and pivots from the physical mechanism, not from arbitrary bbox positions.

Keep XML mesh coordinates aligned with the layout/source mesh coordinates. If a fixed euler correction is required on a geom, validate the resulting initial configuration with an IPC sanity or distance check before stepping.
Drive motion through actuator/DOF controllers such as control_dofs_position_velocity.
Cap PD gains and force ranges in heavy IPC contact scenes to avoid per-step jumps that look like penetration.
For diagnostics, do not trust get_dofs_velocity alone on external articulations whose qpos is recovered from IPC; estimate true angular velocity from step-to-step hinge angle differences when needed.
restrictions: Do not drive external articulations by post-build root/qpos teleports or repeated set_dofs_velocity writes.
Do not provide only visual geometry for contact participants.
Do not let dummy mount geometry intersect or perturb the real contact region even if the dummy is invisible, transparent, tiny, or only intended for IPC bookkeeping.
Do not use ordinary task-contact settings such as contype="1" conaffinity="1" for dummy mount/base geoms that are close enough to touch task objects.

Do not use a unilateral base_collision_mount in the roller gap or at a reachable roller end for FEM/IPC squeeze tasks; route that to XML asset repair/regeneration rather than trying to tune action speed or material stiffness.

Do not assume that whitelisting only the visually named child link is enough when fixed child geoms may be merged into a parent link for IPC collision; validate the actual merge target or include it explicitly.
checks: Search generated XML for mount/base/dummy/fixed geoms and inspect their position, size, contype, and conaffinity relative to the active contact path.
Flag XML as an asset defect if a dummy mount/base geom can collide with the object being manipulated, squeezed, transported, contained, or supported.
Check positive initial clearance from dummy mount/base geoms to all deformables and task-contact bodies, and confirm the mount is far outside the main simulation/contact region.
For roller or squeeze mechanisms, check that no mount/base collision geom lies near the roller gap, roller end where the soft body can expand, or below-gap exit path.
If a soft body suddenly shoots sideways near an end mount or central mount, inspect the XML before blaming the controller; hidden mount contact is a likely asset defect.

If driven clamps, sockets, fingers, paddles, or rollers visibly move but a FEM body remains static, inspect the IPC collision whitelist and fixed-joint merge target links before tuning gains, friction, or material stiffness.

For endpoint-driven FEM rods or ropes, require metrics showing nontrivial FEM vertex displacement, endpoint movement, braid/twist change, and rod-rod/contact evidence; do not accept successful actuator DOFs alone.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when rewriting XML asset requests.
Send to body/action when wiring actuator names, DOF handles, and controller paths.
Send to critic when generated XML morphology cannot plausibly perform the requested contact task.

cards.title: FEM+IPC scenes need one support surface and conservative positive clearances
cards.summary: FEM+IPC initialization should avoid duplicate IPC support planes, coincident contact surfaces, and rotated primitive placements that rely on under-estimated extents.
restrictions: Use exactly one IPC ground/support surface for the same contact region.
If scene.py creates a floor, store it as scene.genesis_static_floor; body.py should reference that entity in actors instead of adding another coincident plane.
Duplicate overlapping IPC planes are invalid initial geometry.
Place FEM primitives with strictly positive initial clearance.
For rotated boxes, compute a conservative half extent such as 0.5 * side * sqrt(3) and stack centers with a positive gap; do not rely on unrotated side/2 when the body has nonzero Euler rotation.
For primitive soft bodies, use tet_resolution from deformable_cfg.
dispatch_hints: Send to scene/body when FEM+IPC bodies or support surfaces are created.
Send to critic when diagnosing initial penetration, World-is-not-valid, or duplicate support geometry.

cards.title: Tune IPC contact resistance and rigid coupling strength within config bounds
cards.summary: IPC contact hardness and rigid-soft coupling tightness may be tuned from deformable_cfg bounds when evidence shows contact sinking, loose clamps, laggy driven rigid bodies, or overly stiff unstable contact.
guidance: Treat ipc_contact_resistance, ipc_constraint_strength_translation, and ipc_constraint_strength_rotation as agent-tunable IPC parameters with config-provided defaults and bounds in deformable_cfg.

Use ipc_contact_resistance_default=1e7 with range [3e6, 1e8]. This parameter controls IPC contact normal resistance/stiffness, not restitution. Increasing it makes contact harder, reduces sinking and visible overlap, and can improve support/contact crispness; excessive values make the system stiffer and may cause jitter, slow convergence, or NaN unless timing/solver quality is also adequate. Decreasing it makes contact softer and more forgiving but can permit visible sinking, mushy support, weak impact response, or apparent penetration.

Use ipc_constraint_strength_translation_default=30 with range [10, 100]. This controls translational soft-transform coupling between Genesis-driven rigid bodies and IPC ABD objects. Increasing it makes driven rigid bodies, clamps, and tools follow translation targets more tightly in IPC and reduces lag/slip; excessive values can inject stiff coupling forces, jitter, ejection, or instability. Decreasing it softens coupling and can improve stability but may make clamps/tools feel loose or delayed.

Use ipc_constraint_strength_rotation_default=30 with range [10, 100]. This controls rotational soft-transform coupling. Increase it for twisting clamps, rotating grippers, paddles, rollers, and articulated links whose IPC contact rotation lags or fails to drive soft bodies. Too high can create torque spikes, jitter, or unstable twist contact; too low can make rotations fail to transfer visibly.

Prefer conservative sweeps: contact_resistance 1e7 -> 3e7 -> 1e8 for sinking contact, or 1e7 -> 3e6 for over-stiff instability; constraint strengths 30 -> 50 -> 100 for loose clamp/coupling, or 30 -> 10 for unstable coupling.
When Opt tunes these parameters, use log scale for ipc_contact_resistance and linear or log scale for the two positive strength values; record requested and effective values in metrics or reports.
restrictions: Do not change FEM E, nu, density, cloth thickness, friction, action velocity, or camera choices as a substitute for tuning IPC contact/coupling response.

Do not pass *_default, *_min, or *_max metadata fields to gs.options.IPCCouplerOptions; only pass the effective values ipc_contact_resistance, ipc_constraint_strength_translation, and ipc_constraint_strength_rotation.
Do not raise coupling strength to hide a wrong coupling mode, disabled collision, missing coupled links, or bad initial overlap; repair ownership and geometry first.
checks: Check whether failure evidence is contact softness/sinking, loose rigid-soft coupling, rotational lag, or over-stiff instability before choosing the tuning direction.
Check generated scene.py maps only effective IPC option values into IPCCouplerOptions.
Check reports or metrics include the effective values if these fields were tuned.
dispatch_hints: Send to Planner and Opt when critic evidence suggests bounded scalar tuning of IPC contact or coupling.
Send to scene/body when writing or repairing IPC option mapping and rigid coupling behavior.
Send to critic when diagnosing whether a failure is structural or parameter-limited.

provenance.motivation: Expose IPC contact resistance and transform-coupling strengths as bounded agent-tunable parameters.

cards.title: IPC scene options should map deformable_cfg values exactly and leave adaptive d_hat to the entrypoint
cards.summary: Generated IPC scenes should construct IPCCouplerOptions from deformable_cfg with the correct prefix stripping, rigid-contact flags, and adaptive contact distance handling.
guidance: If deformable_cfg.ipc_enabled is true for a rigid/articulated scene, route contact through IPC by constructing gs.Scene with gs.options.IPCCouplerOptions.

Map IPC values from deformable_cfg by stripping the ipc_ config prefix before passing Genesis option names, such as ipc_contact_d_hat to contact_d_hat and ipc_constraint_strength_translation to constraint_strength_translation.

Map only actual Genesis IPCCouplerOptions fields, including ipc_newton_*, ipc_n_linesearch_iterations, ipc_linesearch_report_energy, ipc_linear_system_*, ipc_contact_enable, ipc_contact_d_hat, ipc_contact_friction_enable, ipc_contact_resistance, ipc_contact_eps_velocity, ipc_contact_constitution, ipc_collision_detection_method, ipc_cfl_enable, ipc_sanity_check_enable, ipc_constraint_strength_translation, ipc_constraint_strength_rotation, ipc_enable_rigid_ground_contact, ipc_enable_rigid_rigid_contact, ipc_two_way_coupling, ipc_enable_rigid_dofs_sync, and ipc_free_base_driven_by_ipc.
Do not pass deformable_cfg range metadata keys ending in _default, _min, or _max to gs.options.IPCCouplerOptions; those keys are available to Planner/Opt as tuning bounds only.
Do not pass ipc_contact_d_hat_adaptive to Genesis; it is a code-agent runtime switch and the generated entrypoint resolves it into ipc_contact_d_hat before create_scene.

When ipc_contact_d_hat_adaptive is true, the entrypoint computes clamp(min(0.2 * median_mesh_edge_or_bbox_feature), 1e-5 * max_bbox_diag, 2e-3 * max_bbox_diag) over generated meshes, user/repo meshes, primitive-derived meshes, collision-enabled XML primitive geoms, direct primitive morphs, bbox-only primitive/XML/URDF manifest entries, and planner_output asset_requests as fallback.

For rigid IPC scenes, set enable_rigid_rigid_contact from deformable_cfg.ipc_enable_rigid_rigid_contact. Keep it true when rigid bodies should collide with each other through IPC; keep it false when IPC should only handle rigid-soft contact or selected articulated contacts and Genesis's rigid solver should avoid delegated pairs.

Rigid participants in IPC scenes should use gs.materials.Rigid with appropriate IPC coupling fields such as coup_type and coup_friction; the generic deformable_cfg friction may be used as a rigid coupling fallback when relevant.
dispatch_hints: Send to scene/body when constructing gs.Scene or materials for IPC-enabled cases.
Send to critic when diagnosing missing IPC options, wrong rigid-contact flags, or hardcoded IPC defaults.

cards.title: Relax IPC stiffness only when it preserves the requested physical meaning
cards.summary: Lower IPC stiffness/contact/coupling values can improve stability only if the task does not require stiff support, hard clamping, strong constraints, or shape preservation.
guidance: Relax IPC stiffness, contact resistance, or coupling strength only when the requested visual behavior and physical semantics remain intact.
If the task asks for stiff support, hard clamps, rigid shape preservation, strong endpoint constraints, or precise rigid-soft transfer, do not solve the failure by weakening those requirements.
When relaxation is appropriate, prefer small bounded decreases and re-check the target behavior rather than jumping to the minimum values.
If lowering stiffness removes the required effect, route repair to geometry, coupling mode, clamp design, timing, solver quality, or asset topology instead.
restrictions: Do not relax FEM E/nu, IPC contact resistance, or transform-coupling strength just to make an impossible or badly staged setup pass.
Do not call a result successful if relaxed settings avoid instability by eliminating the visible contact, clamp, twist, support, or deformation requested by the prompt.
checks: Compare the relaxed result against the original prompt: is the required stiffness/contact/constraint still visible?
Check whether a structural repair would preserve semantics better than lowering stiffness.
dispatch_hints: Send to Planner, Critic, and Opt when considering a stability fix that weakens IPC contact or coupling.
Send to body/scene when repair instructions mention lowering IPC stiffness-like parameters.

provenance.motivation: Prevent stability repairs from weakening the core physical behavior requested by the prompt.

cards.title: Tune non-IPC rigid collision response through contact solver parameters only
cards.summary: For ordinary rigid Genesis contact, collision bounce/compliance should be adjusted through local contact solver parameters such as sol_params, not by changing task energy, friction, geometry, or unrelated global hyperparameters.
guidance: Use ordinary rigid contact solver parameters when the requested behavior specifically needs a more or less elastic collision response in non-IPC rigid mode.

Genesis rigid contact sol_params are (timeconst, dampratio, dmin, dmax, width, mid, power). The internal note in genesis.utils.geom.default_solver_params states that timeconst controls velocity damping and dampratio controls spring stiffness, despite the name.

To make collision less dead while staying within contact-only tuning, reduce contact velocity damping by using a larger positive timeconst, and increase elastic restoring stiffness by using a smaller positive dampratio. Keep dampratio strictly positive.
To make collision less bouncy or reduce jitter, increase dampratio or use a shorter/more dissipative timeconst, while keeping timeconst above the solver safety minimum enforced by Genesis.
Prefer local per-geom set_sol_params for task-critical colliders instead of broad global changes; record any effective sol_params in metrics or reports when they were important.
restrictions: Do not alter initial velocity, actuation strength, gravity, mass, shape, FEM/cloth material properties, or friction to satisfy this collision-elasticity card.

Do not change pre-specified runtime hyperparameters such as sim_dt, sim_substeps, solver iteration counts, or global rigid solver options under this card; route those as separate hyperparameter or execution-quality work.
Do not set dampratio to zero; Genesis forbids zero because it removes the elastic restoring force.
Do not use non-IPC sol_params advice for IPC scenes; IPC contact should use the IPC tuning card instead.
checks: Check the case is in rigid physics mode rather than rigid_ipc or fem_ipc.
Check any sol_params change affects colliding geoms that actually participate in the requested contact.
Check the video for both semantic correctness and absence of excessive jitter/ejection after tuning.
dispatch_hints: Send to body/action when local rigid contact response needs collision-only tuning.
Send to critic and opt when a rigid contact result is close but collision response is too dead, too soft, or too unstable.

provenance.motivation: Keep non-IPC collision elasticity fixes scoped to collision-contact parameters rather than unrelated task or hyperparameter changes.

cards.title: Choose rigid IPC coupling mode from the physical role
cards.summary: Rigid IPC scenes should choose ipc_only, two_way_soft_constraint, or external_articulation based on whether IPC, Genesis controls, or articulated DOFs own the motion.
guidance: Use ipc_only for passive free rigid props whose motion should mainly come from IPC gravity/contact.
Use two_way_soft_constraint for Genesis-driven bodies or selected robot links that need IPC contact while keeping Genesis controls.

For two_way_soft_constraint, constraint_strength_translation and constraint_strength_rotation tune how tightly IPC tracks Genesis: higher is stiffer and less laggy, lower is softer and usually more forgiving.
Use external_articulation for fixed-base MJCF/URDF mechanisms whose joints should participate in IPC contact.

When ipc_enable_rigid_rigid_contact / enable_rigid_rigid_contact is true for a heavy rigid-contact scene, treat the scene as pure IPC rigid-rigid contact: use ipc_only for passive rigid bodies and external_articulation for actively driven articulated bodies that contact IPC-owned rigid bodies.
In heavy rigid-rigid IPC contact scenes, do not create rigid bodies with two_way_soft_constraint unless there is a specific non-heavy-contact reason to do so.
Use coup_links only for two_way_soft_constraint, and use it to couple just the contacting links such as gripper fingers rather than putting an entire robot into IPC.
Choose explicit coupling modes when contact behavior depends on the choice; do not rely on auto-selection for important objects.
restrictions: Do not pose-write, velocity-write, force-drive, hidden-weld, or directly attach passive ipc_only payloads after initialization.
Do not rely on Genesis rigid contact as a fallback for ipc_only objects.

Avoid mixing two_way_soft_constraint and ipc_only in heavy interlocking contact because soft transform constraints and Genesis/IPC synchronization can create inconsistent states, sudden ejection, or crashes.
Do not use ipc_only for articulated objects; use external_articulation or selected two_way_soft_constraint links depending on the physical role.
dispatch_hints: Send to body when assigning material coupling modes.
Send to action when an actuator-driven mechanism contacts IPC-owned objects.
Send to critic when contact ownership or state synchronization looks inconsistent.

cards.title: Use real dimensions or uniform scale instead of hidden non-uniform stretching
cards.summary: Generated assets and primitives should normally express shape through dimensions, geometry, XML geom sizes, or regenerated assets, not unequal per-axis scale values.
restrictions: Do not use unequal per-axis scale values unless the task explicitly requests an exceptional anisotropic scaling operation.
Use primitive dimensions, generated-asset geometry, XML geom sizes, or a regenerated asset to express elongated or flattened shapes.
Use uniform scalar scale for unit conversion, global sizing, and layout fitting.
checks: Search planner outputs and generated source for scale=(sx, sy, sz) with unequal positive components.
If non-uniform scale is truly needed, require the plan or worker report to document the reason.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner for asset_requests and metadata updates.
Send to body/rendering when workers instantiate meshes or XML assets.

cards.title: Reduce active control stress before changing physics
cards.summary: When contact-rich simulations become slow, unstable, or eject objects under aggressive actuation, first reduce the commanded controller targets, gains, force limits, and external-force magnitudes before changing material or contact physics.
guidance: If a simulation becomes very slow or unstable after contact, clamping, squeezing, twisting, rolling, pressing, or insertion begins, first inspect the active commands sent by action.py rather than immediately changing material stiffness, friction, IPC contact settings, or geometry.

Reduce commanded position, angle, velocity, compression, insertion depth, overtravel, lateral offset, vertical offset, roller speed, press depth, push distance, or twist target to the smallest value that still demonstrates the requested behavior.

Lower PD or actuator aggressiveness before changing the physical world: reduce kp, retune kv for damping rather than impact, and cap force_range or torque limits so the controller cannot inject more stress than the contact mechanism can transmit.

For direct external-force or impulse actuation, reduce magnitude, spread force over more steps, and log the applied force count and total impulse so Critic can distinguish gentle physical driving from a hidden outcome force.

Use longer smooth ramps, phase gates, and dwell periods so contacts seat before the next high-stress phase begins. A slower actuation schedule is usually preferable to a stiffer contact or stronger clamp.

When late-stage failure appears after an initially successful motion, tune the late-stage actuation signal first: shorten or weaken overtwist, compression, lateral bend, squeeze, release, or retreat phases before changing FEM E, material friction, contact resistance, or IPC d_hat.
For Opt, expose controller targets, kp, kv, force or torque limits, ramp durations, and late-phase amplitudes as tunable variables when failure evidence points to over-actuation.
restrictions: Do not fix aggressive-controller failures by disabling collision, IPC contact, self-contact, or physical coupling.
Do not first make the material softer, less frictional, or less contact-resistant when the evidence shows that commanded targets, gains, or external forces are the source of high stress.
Do not hide a late-stage instability by ending the video before the over-actuated phase fails unless the task explicitly only requires the earlier stable behavior.
Do not replace unstable physical actuation with direct task-object state writes, hidden attachments, suction, or target-following forces.
checks: Compare the time of failure with action phase transitions and commanded target magnitudes.
Check controller logs or source for large target jumps, high kp, high force_range, high torque limits, large external forces, or late-stage overtravel/compression/bending commands.
If the scene is slow but not crashed, inspect whether the slowdown begins when active commands create dense contact or high preload.
For endpoint clamps, rollers, presses, and grippers, check whether the task object slips, ejects, or deforms unnaturally only after the command exceeds the contact mechanism's retention capacity.
After reducing active control stress, verify that the target behavior still appears in visual evidence and metrics instead of only becoming passive or under-actuated.
dispatch_hints: Send to action when writing or repairing staged controller schedules, PD targets, force limits, or external-force commands.
Send to critic when a run is visually plausible early but fails after stronger late-stage actuation.
Send to opt when remaining failures look tunable through controller targets, gains, force limits, torque limits, force magnitudes, or phase durations.
Send to planner for contact-rich tasks that mention squeezing, twisting, clamping, rolling, pressing, insertion, pushing, or grasping.

provenance.motivation: The twisting-rods run succeeded early but failed after high twist/compression/lateral actuation pulled FEM endpoints out of contact clamps; reducing active commands is the preferred first repair over changing material physics.

cards.title: Action schedules should stage approach, contact, hold, release, and settle phases
cards.summary: Generated action code should use physically plausible staged controls with bounded gains and forces instead of abrupt jumps, teleports, or one-step outcome forcing.
guidance: For manipulation tasks, prefer explicit phases such as approach, pre-contact alignment, contact/closure, hold/transport, release, and settle.
Use smooth ramps or bounded target updates for position, velocity, or force controls instead of instant large target jumps.
Record phase transitions, commanded targets, and key actuator values in event logs or metrics.
When contact is delicate, tune controller gains and force limits conservatively before using Opt.
If the mechanism is XML/MJCF articulated, preserve the actuator/DOF control path exposed by body.py.
restrictions: Do not move passive task objects directly to compensate for poor controller timing.
Do not use hidden attachments, suction, or artificial object-following forces unless explicitly requested by the task.
Do not hide an unstable schedule by ending the video before the result can fail.
checks: Check that task-object motion follows from actuator, contact, or force interaction rather than direct object state writes.
Check that final metrics include enough timing evidence to repair early or late contact, release, or settling failures.
dispatch_hints: Send to action for grasping, pushing, pressing, releasing, catching, balancing, or robot-control tasks.
Send to opt when remaining failure looks like timing, gain, target, or force-limit tuning.
Send to critic when evaluating whether the control method is physically plausible.

provenance.motivation: Add reusable staged-control experience for action worker and Opt/Critic review.

cards.title: Controller phase gates should fail soft instead of deadlocking the task
cards.summary: Action controllers that wait for contact, stability, trapping, alignment, or pressure thresholds should use robust gates with timeouts, fallback transitions, and explicit logs so one missed detector does not erase the required behavior.
guidance: For every required behavior phase, define both the preferred evidence gate and a bounded fallback that keeps the physical task progressing if the detector is noisy or slightly missed.

Gate on robust combinations such as contact dwell, distance trend, actuator target reached within tolerance, low relative velocity, or elapsed time rather than a single brittle equality or one-frame force sample.
Record each phase transition, gate reason, blocked-count, current gate residual, fallback use, and the final phase reached in event logs or metrics.
When a gate fails, prefer conservative physical progress such as slower approach, extra dwell, partial release, or reduced-speed retry rather than holding the same command forever.
Use Opt for continuous thresholds and durations only after source code exposes meaningful phase metrics and the schedule can complete all required phases.
restrictions: Do not make a required task phase conditional on an impossible or overly strict detector with no timeout.
Do not report task success when required phase timestamps such as release_time, lift_start_time, catch_time, or retreat_time are null.
Do not let adaptive recovery loops replace the requested behavior for most of the video without marking the episode as failed or incomplete.
checks: Check that all prompt-critical phases have non-null timestamps and occur in the intended order.
Check whether blocked_counts, gate residuals, or fallback flags show that the controller spent the episode waiting for a gate that never opened.
Check whether visual evidence shows the requested physical sequence rather than a static hold, repeated recovery loop, or skipped phase.
Check that fallback transitions still preserve physical causality and do not directly move passive task objects.
dispatch_hints: Send to action for staged controllers with contact, release, trapping, balancing, catching, or alignment gates.
Send to critic when a completed render is missing required phases despite apparently valid source code.
Send to opt when gate thresholds and dwell times are exposed as compact continuous parameters.

provenance.motivation: Multiple controllers deadlocked because a contact or stability gate never triggered, leaving release, lift, catch, or locomotion phases absent.

cards.title: Task object motion must come from modeled physics or explicit controls
cards.summary: After initialization, passive task objects should move because of dynamics, contact, joints, actuators, or requested physical effects, not because generated code writes them toward the goal.
restrictions: Do not move passive task objects after initialization with pose, qpos, qvel, velocity, or root-state writes.
Do not add hidden constraints, target-following proxy forces, or outcome-directed external forces to make an object succeed.

Direct state writes such as setting root pose, qpos, entity pose, DOF position, or velocity are acceptable for initialization only; after stepping begins, movement should be driven by actuator commands, DOF controllers, motors, external forces/torques, or pre-step initial velocities.
External forces are allowed only when they are part of the requested scene physics or a clearly modeled mechanism such as wind, thrust, magnetism, springs, launch impulse, or actuator force.
Allowed external forces must be applied to the physically appropriate body, recorded in metrics, and must not directly encode the desired task outcome.
If attachment-like behavior is needed, model it through geometry, joints, contacts, actuator force, or an explicitly requested physical mechanism.
If the current assets cannot express the physical mechanism, route repair to the owning source instead of hiding the gap.

For XML/MJCF articulated assets, repairs should preserve the designed actuator/DOF control path or request a body/asset contract fix that exposes a controllable joint; do not recommend mid-simulation pose teleportation as a repair.
checks: Search generated action/body code for post-build direct state writes to passive task objects.
Compare numeric success with visual/source evidence; reject success if object-following assistance is the main cause.
Identify which entities are controlled, which entities are passive task objects, and which APIs modify them during simulation.
dispatch_hints: Send to action when controls or schedules may directly write task objects.
Send to critic and opt whenever a numeric objective could be satisfied by a non-physical shortcut.

cards.title: Make prompted motion visibly legible
cards.summary: Prompted displacements, rotations, compression, bending, collision, rebound, sliding, and wrapping should be plainly visible in the ordinary render unless the prompt imposes a subtle-motion constraint.
guidance: Actions explicitly requested in the prompt should be obvious from a normal video: translation, rotation, compression, bending, collision, rebound, sliding, twisting, and wrapping should not remain only tiny numerical changes.

When the prompt does not require subtle or delicate motion, increase actuation stroke, speed difference, initial spacing, collision speed, or deformation amplitude enough that a viewer can directly see the behavior.

When the prompt does not require stillness, subtle motion, or an early settled rest state, tune non-semantic parameters such as actuation timing, speed, travel distance, damping, release height, or contact speed so meaningful task-relevant motion occupies more of the video duration.
Use physical causes for larger motion: actuator targets, force schedules, release heights, initial clearances, and contact paths. Do not direct-write passive object states after initialization.
Critic should flag cases where the required action technically exists but is too small or too brief to judge.
restrictions: Do not amplify motion by teleporting, pose-writing, disabling collision, scaling objects mid-run, or faking deformation.
Do not extend motion by adding unrelated actions, changing the requested physical story, or preventing a required final settled state.
Do not increase motion so much that it violates task constraints, leaves the camera frame, or destroys a required delicate/stable outcome.
checks: Inspect the video at ordinary playback speed and confirm the prompted behavior is visible without reading metrics.
Check whether the clip spends unnecessary time nearly static before or after the requested behavior, unless that stillness is part of the prompt.
Check metrics for displacement/rotation/deformation magnitudes when available, but keep video legibility as the main evidence.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner/action for tasks with explicit dynamic verbs.
Send to critic when a result is physically plausible but the requested behavior is hard to see.
Send to opt when remaining failure is a bounded amplitude, timing, or actuation-strength issue.

provenance.motivation: Make explicitly requested motion visible enough for dataset and debug-card review.

cards.title: Release before retreat and prove the object no longer follows the robot
cards.summary: Placement and handoff controllers should separate release, support-transfer, vertical clearance, lateral retreat, and post-release observation so the robot does not drag or secretly support the passive task object.
guidance: Use explicit phases for carry or placement tasks: approach, seat or align, open or release, hold still while support transfers, lift vertically to clearance, retreat laterally, then observe the passive object without contact.
After opening a gripper or removing a support blade, keep the robot nearly still long enough to verify that the object remains on the intended passive support rather than following the end effector.
Prefer vertical clearance before lateral retreat for delicate stacks, cards, arches, trays, cups, and final blocks; lateral motion while still close is a common source of dragging.

Record event-log timestamps and metrics for release_time, support_transfer_time, first_stable_clearance_step, robot_clear_time, minimum gripper-object distance after release, and object displacement after release.
If the remaining failure is close and physically plausible, expose release dwell, lift distance, lift speed, lateral retreat speed, and gripper opening target as Opt parameters.
restrictions: Do not count a task as successful while the robot, blade, gripper, or hidden constraint is still supporting the object that should be passive.
Do not begin lateral retreat before release and vertical clearance evidence exists for tasks where small contact forces can topple, drag, or displace the object.
Do not directly move the passive task object to hide a late or failed release.
checks: Check whether release_time, support_clear_time, robot_clear_time, and first_stable_clearance_step are non-null and ordered correctly.
Check visual evidence that the robot is separated from the object during the final observation window.
Check whether object_followed_gripper, robot_supporting_at_end, top_card_displacement_after_release, final_block_displacement, or similar metrics reveal dragging.
Check that post-release success is judged after the robot has retreated, not during the placement contact itself.
dispatch_hints: Send to action for grasp, placement, block/card/arch/stack balance, cup placement, handoff, and delicate release tasks.
Send to opt when the scene is structurally correct but release dwell, clearance, or retreat timing needs tuning.
Send to critic when deciding whether a rendered placement is really passive after release.

provenance.motivation: Several precise-control runs failed because the robot retreated before stable release, dragging the final object or leaving it robot-supported.

cards.title: Implement slow motion without lowering output FPS
cards.summary: Slow-motion requests should keep output FPS readable while slowing the physical action schedule and increasing captured temporal detail, rather than skipping more simulation steps per frame.
guidance: In the current code_agent render semantics, render_every_n_steps means how many simulation steps are skipped between captured frames. Increasing it while keeping output FPS fixed makes physical time advance faster per video second, not slower.
For slow motion, keep output FPS fixed, slow the action schedule or velocities, and reduce render_every_n_steps or increase total captured frames so intermediate states are visible.
If slowing action and capturing more frames is still insufficient, reduce sim_dt and increase steps accordingly so the same physical behavior is resolved with more simulation samples.
Preserve requested duration semantics explicitly: distinguish physical behavior duration from output video playback speed in the plan and reports.
restrictions: Do not create slow motion by lowering render_fps to a choppy value.
Do not increase render_every_n_steps as the primary slow-motion mechanism under the current renderer semantics.
Do not shorten or truncate the requested behavior to make it appear slower.
checks: Check timing.json, render_stats.json, and action schedule values for FPS, render_every_n_steps, sim_dt, steps, and captured frame count.
Inspect the video around fast contacts or impacts for temporal aliasing.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner/action/rendering whenever the prompt mentions slow motion or when fast contact is visually aliased.
Send to critic when playback speed, FPS, or render cadence makes the behavior hard to judge.

provenance.motivation: Correct the prior slow-motion guidance for render_every_n_steps semantics.

cards.title: Soft-body tasks require real FEM+IPC when deformable generation is enabled
cards.summary: When a prompt requires soft-body or FEM cloth behavior, generated source should use real FEM entities/materials and IPC evidence, not rigid substitutes or unsupported MPM/PBD/SPH shortcuts.
restrictions: When deformable_config.enabled is false, generated source must not use FEM materials, FEM entities, or deformation-only APIs.
If the prompt fundamentally requires soft-body or deformation behavior while deformable is disabled, Planner should finish inconclusive rather than write a rigid-body substitute.
When deformable_config.ipc_enabled is false, generated source must not instantiate gs.options.IPCCouplerOptions.
When deformable_config.enabled is false and ipc_enabled is true, IPC is allowed only for rigid/articulated contact; fail fake deformable behavior.
Do not use MPM, PBD, SPH, PBD cloth shortcuts, or rigid-only substitutes for soft-body prompts in this pipeline.

When a prompt explicitly requires thin-shell cloth, use gs.materials.FEM.Cloth with a ready cloth_mesh surface asset and IPC, not volumetric FEM Elastic unless the task has been rewritten as a thick solid.
Do not fake deformation through repeated FEM position or velocity writes after stepping starts.
Require FEM material/entity construction, explicit E/nu/rho/friction_mu choices, config-driven FEM/IPC/tet parameters, and deformation evidence.
Fail hardcoded FEM/IPC defaults, missing E/nu/rho, missing friction_mu, attempts to read deformable_cfg["fem_friction_mu"], and mid-simulation FEM position/velocity writes that fake deformation.
checks: Check generated source for FEM materials/entities and IPC coupler options when deformable generation is enabled.
Check metrics and video for visible deformation such as wobble, compression, bending, collapse, or spread.
dispatch_hints: Send to body/action when a soft-body task is being implemented.
Send to critic when rejecting rigid-only or visually faked soft-body results.
Send to opt when material or contact parameters are tunable but the source already contains real FEM hooks.

cards.title: Prefer verified FEM vertex constraints for boundary control
cards.summary: FEM+IPC soft-body and cloth boundary actions should prefer the verified FEMEntity vertex-constraint path for pinned or driven endpoints, edges, rims, and handle patches, while using contact-driven tools for contact, rolling, squeezing, impact, or force-driven motion.
guidance: For FEM+IPC endpoint, edge, rim, or patch control, prefer FEMEntity.set_vertex_constraints over contact-only workaround clamps when the prompt calls for pinned boundaries, fixed clamps, moving clamps, endpoint constraints, driven rims, or attached handles.

Enable this path with gs.options.FEMOptions(enable_vertex_constraints=True). Under IPCCoupler these are soft position constraints, so choose stiffness deliberately and verify constrained vertices remain close enough to their targets.

Use target_poss for fixed world pins or moving world targets, update_constraint_targets for animated endpoints, link=... for vertices following a visible rigid link, and target_entity/target_verts_idx_local only when one-way FEM-to-FEM target following is intended.

Constrain only the boundary vertices that correspond to the physical clamp, pin, handle, rim, socket, or support. Use a small selected patch when needed for stability; do not constrain broad interior regions or the whole object to sculpt the final shape.
When using link-based constraints, make the clamp or handle visible and place its geometry outside, beside, or around the constrained boundary rather than interpenetrating the cloth or soft body.

If a cloth or deformable-body boundary is constrained to a visible rigid clamp, handle, ring, rod, socket, link, or target body, make the rendered attachment look physically connected: the constrained boundary should touch, sit inside, wrap around, or be covered by a visible connector region with no floating gap or invisible tether. Any numerical offset used for stability should be hidden by connector geometry rather than leaving the soft body visibly suspended away from the object.
Treat link-based vertex constraints as one-way target following. Do not claim that they provide stitch-like bidirectional coupling, weld reaction forces, or load transfer back into the rigid body.

For tasks where motion should arise from contact, friction, rolling, squeezing, collision, free fall, rebound, or distributed physical forces, use actuated rigid contacts or the verified external-force path instead of vertex constraints.

If a task needs true bidirectional rigid-soft attachment, load transfer, or stitch-style behavior, do not present soft position constraints as sufficient; use visible contact geometry if acceptable, or mark the missing coupling as a limitation.

Record deformation evidence tied to the actuation channel, such as constrained vertex target error, selected vertex count, endpoint displacement, FEM COM displacement, bbox change, height ratio, spread, surface marker displacement, contact count, and max/min vertex displacement.
Treat scheduled impulses, disturbances, or constraints as incomplete unless metrics prove that they were applied and changed the soft body state.
restrictions: Do not use private or guessed UIPC/FEM force slots whose shape or semantics are not verified by a successful run.
Do not directly edit FEM vertices, poses, or qpos after initialization to create the requested soft-body motion.
Do not use vertex constraints as hidden goal-following shortcuts for passive regions or for the final object shape.
Do not use FEMEntity.set_vertex_constraints as a substitute for distributed physical forces such as wind, thrust, gravity-like loads, or impact impulses.
Do not claim a link-based FEM vertex constraint is a physical stitch, weld, or bidirectional rigid-soft coupling unless such coupling has actually been implemented and verified.

Do not constrain FEM or cloth vertices to a visible clamp, link, or body while leaving a visible air gap, floating endpoint, or invisible tether. If the prompt names a clamp, handle, ring, or rigid body, the soft boundary must visually meet that object.
Do not mark a run successful when force_attempts is nonzero but force_applications is zero, or when the deformable COM/deformation metrics remain unchanged.
Do not mark endpoint-clamp scenes successful when actuator DOFs reach their targets but FEM vertex displacement, endpoint displacement, twist/braid, or contact evidence remain near zero.
checks: Check that gs.options.FEMOptions(enable_vertex_constraints=True) is set when FEMEntity.set_vertex_constraints is used under IPCCoupler.
Check that constrained vertices are boundary, handle, rim, socket, or pin vertices, not arbitrary interior or whole-body vertices.
Check logs and metrics for selected vertex count, target updates, max constrained-vertex error, and FEM/cloth deformation response.
For visible clamps or handles, inspect frames for initial interpenetration and make sure the rigid visual clamp is placed beside or around the constrained boundary rather than inside the soft body.

For every visible FEM/cloth attachment, inspect start, middle, and end frames and verify the constrained boundary remains visually connected to the clamp, handle, ring, link, or target body with no air gap, no floating endpoint, and no misleading invisible tether.
Check metrics and frames for actual deformation caused by the intended physical channel.
For twisting or braiding rods, compare initial and final frames plus numeric braid/twist and RMS vertex displacement; static rods with moving clamps indicate a body/collision-coupling defect.
Reject static soft bodies with moving visual clamps when constrained FEM/cloth vertices do not follow the intended target.
dispatch_hints: Send to body when the scene needs visible clamps, handles, rims, support geometry, or boundary vertex selection.
Send to action for animated constraint targets, moving endpoint schedules, link-following targets, and force/contact actuation schedules.
Send to critic when logs claim scheduled FEM actuation but visible or numeric deformation is absent.
Send to opt when constraint stiffness, selected boundary patch, target trajectory, material parameters, or contact/friction settings need tuning.

provenance.motivation: Older FEM runs scheduled disturbances or constraints through unsupported channels, but FEMEntity.set_vertex_constraints is now implemented and render-tested for FEM and cloth under IPCCoupler, including fixed world targets, moving endpoints, static rigid-link targets, and moving rigid-link targets.

cards.title: FEM cloth uses IPC shell meshes, not PBD or volumetric tets
cards.summary: Thin cloth tasks should use gs.materials.FEM.Cloth with ready procedural cloth_mesh surface assets and IPC contact; PBD cloth and volumetric FEM substitutes remain out of scope unless the task is explicitly rewritten.
guidance: Request cloth geometry as asset_type cloth_mesh or one of cloth_mesh_square, cloth_mesh_rectangle, cloth_mesh_cylinder, or cloth_mesh_sphere; use the resulting asset_manifest entry with source_type cloth_mesh.
For cloth asset_request bbox, all three components must be positive; use a small positive thickness dimension such as 0.001 for flat square or rectangle sheets instead of 0.0.

Every asset_request includes cloth_target_edge_length; set it to null for non-cloth assets or default cloth resolution, or set a positive meter value to control cloth primitive mesh resolution per asset.

Choose cloth_target_edge_length relative to bbox size and contact needs, then check validation.cloth_mesh.target_edge_length, median_edge_length, and face_count; the generator may coarsen relative to the target when the configured max face budget would be exceeded.
Instantiate cloth with gs.morphs.Mesh(file=runtime_path, scale=scale or 1.0, file_meshes_are_zup=True) and gs.materials.FEM.Cloth.
Choose E, nu, rho, thickness, bending_stiffness, and friction_mu from deformable_cfg cloth defaults/ranges; do not use tet_resolution for FEM.Cloth.
For square and rectangle cloth, start with moderate grid resolution and positive clearance from rigid colliders.
For cylindrical and spherical cloth shells, keep the mesh coarse enough for IPC contact and avoid initial self-intersection or near-coincident folds.
Use IPC-compatible rigid contact participants such as ipc_only fixed floors/obstacles when they should collide with cloth.
restrictions: Do not instantiate gs.materials.PBD.Cloth or gs.options.PBDOptions in code_agent cloth tasks.
Do not use Genesis built-in meshes/cloth.obj; use ready cloth_mesh assets generated into the case workspace.
Do not pass tet_resolution to cloth morphs or route cloth meshes through the generated_mesh watertight/tetgen repair path.
Do not fake cloth motion with rigid sheets, direct vertex pose edits after stepping starts, hidden constraints, or keyframed folding.
checks: Check generated source for gs.materials.FEM.Cloth and absence of PBD cloth APIs.
Check asset_manifest for a ready source_type cloth_mesh entry whose runtime_path exists and whose face count is within budget.
Check initial layout for positive clearance relative to cloth thickness and ipc_contact_d_hat.
Check video and metrics for visible bending, draping, folding, or shell deformation rather than rigid-sheet motion.
dispatch_hints: Send to Planner when a prompt mentions cloth, sheet, ribbon, drape, garment-like fabric, thin shell, or twisting cloth.
Send to Body when implementing FEM.Cloth entities from cloth_mesh assets.
Send to Critic when rejecting PBD, volumetric-only, rigid-sheet, or keyframed cloth approximations.

cards.title: FEM evidence should record deformation proxies without oversampling expensive state
cards.summary: Generated FEM tasks should expose useful deformation evidence such as COM, bounding boxes, height, spread, and sparse vertex-state samples while avoiding per-step expensive get_state calls by default.
guidance: FEM state sampling can use entity.get_state(); convert state.pos/state.vel tensors to CPU/numpy/lists before writing JSON.
Event logs and metrics should include COM, bbox/height, spread, and a simple deformation proxy for soft-body tasks.
entity.get_state() for FEM bodies can be expensive. Sample FEM vertex state sparsely for metrics/evidence, not on every physics step by default.
dispatch_hints: Send to body/action when FEM metrics or event logs are implemented.
Send to critic when checking whether a soft-body result has enough deformation evidence.
Send to opt when FEM material/contact variables need observable effective metrics.

cards.title: Use verified libuipc external-force attributes for FEM forces
cards.summary: For FEM+IPC scenes that truly need direct physical force on a deformable body, register uipc.constitution.FiniteElementExternalForce on FEM IPC geometries before IPC world initialization, then update vertices.external_force and vertices.is_constrained with shape checks, sync, and motion evidence.
guidance: Use direct FEM external force only when force is part of the requested physical scene or a clearly modeled disturbance. Prefer FEMEntity.set_vertex_constraints for pinned or driven boundary conditions, and prefer contact-driven rigid tools for rollers, plates, grippers, impacts, squeezing, and contact mechanisms.
Genesis does not expose a high-level FEMEntity.apply_external_force API for IPCCoupler. Do not call rigid_solver.apply_links_external_force for FEM bodies.

Register uipc.constitution.FiniteElementExternalForce after IPCCoupler has created FEM geometries in coupler._ipc_scene and before coupler._ipc_world.init(...). A generated simulation can do this by patching IPCCoupler._add_fem_entities_to_ipc before scene.build().

Find target FEM geometries with uipc.backend.SceneVisitor(coupler._ipc_scene), uipc.geometry.SimplicialComplexSlot, and genesis.engine.couplers.ipc_coupler.utils.read_ipc_geometry_metadata; apply this card only to metadata entries with solver_type == 'fem' unless cloth force support is separately verified.

Create and insert a FiniteElementExternalForce instance in coupler._ipc_constitution_tabular, then call external_force.apply_to(fem_geom, np.zeros(3, dtype=np.float64)). Verify that fem_geom.vertices().find('external_force') and fem_geom.vertices().find('is_constrained') both exist.

Normalize the external_force attribute to a per-vertex (N, 3) writable view. In the local smoke test, pyuipc exposed vertices.external_force as shape (N, 3, 1), so generated code should handle shape variants such as (N, 3, 1), (N, 3), (3, N), or a flat 3N layout instead of assuming one fixed shape.

When applying a total force to a selected vertex patch, divide by the selected vertex count, write that per-vertex vector into external_force for selected vertices, set the corresponding is_constrained mask entries to 1, and zero force/mask entries for all unforced vertices.
After writing force attributes, call coupler._ipc_world.sync() before scene.step(), or register a coupler._ipc_animator callback that updates force attributes before IPC advance.
Clear or update force and mask attributes after each step unless the intended physics is a persistent force field.

Record force evidence including attempted force count, successful application count, selected vertex count, external_force shape, mask nonzero count, total force or impulse, per-vertex force, and FEM COM/bbox/vertex displacement.
restrictions: Do not rely on scene.add_force_field for FEM+IPC external force unless that path has been separately verified in the current simulator.

Do not use FEMEntity.set_vertex_constraints as the external-force path for distributed forces or impulses. It is allowed for explicit pinned or driven FEM/cloth boundary conditions under IPCCoupler when gs.options.FEMOptions(enable_vertex_constraints=True) is set and target-tracking evidence is recorded.
Do not write private or guessed UIPC force slots. Require a successful attribute check for vertices.external_force and vertices.is_constrained before claiming force support.
Do not use hidden goal-following forces, target-tracking proxy forces, or forces that directly encode the desired outcome rather than a physical mechanism.
Do not mark FEM force actuation successful unless force_applications is nonzero, selected vertices are nonzero, and FEM state evidence shows corresponding COM, bbox, or vertex displacement.
checks: Confirm FiniteElementExternalForce was registered before IPC world initialization.
Confirm the target FEM geometry was found through IPC metadata and not by fragile object-order assumptions.
Confirm external_force and is_constrained attributes exist on vertices.
Confirm external_force shape was normalized correctly; the verified local shape is (N, 3, 1).
Confirm coupler._ipc_world.sync() or an IPC animator callback transfers updated attributes before IPC advance.
Compare a no-force baseline or pre-force frames against forced frames when possible; the smoke test moved the FEM COM by +0.0186076 m in x over 30 steps while the no-force baseline stayed at 0.
Inspect logs for force_event_count, applied_vertices, mask_nonzero, total_force, per_vertex_force, and FEM COM displacement.
dispatch_hints: Send to body when the generated scene must install libuipc FEM force attributes during IPC build.
Send to action when writing force schedules, force masks, impulses, persistent fields, or animator callbacks.
Send to critic when a run claims FEM external force was applied or when a soft body remains static despite scheduled force.
Send to opt when force magnitude, selected patch, impulse duration, ramp profile, or clearing schedule are tunable physical parameters.
Send to planner for FEM+IPC tasks that explicitly mention wind, thrust, impulse, disturbance, direct force, or force-driven soft-body motion.

verified_details: uipc.constitution.FiniteElementExternalForce is available in the local pyuipc package.
FiniteElementExternalForce.apply_to creates vertices.external_force and vertices.is_constrained on a volumetric FEM SimplicialComplex.
The observed vertices.external_force layout in the smoke test is (N, 3, 1).
Writing +x force to all 1657 FEM vertices for 30 steps and calling coupler._ipc_world.sync() produced +0.0186076 m FEM COM displacement over a no-force baseline of 0.0 m.

cards.title: FEM material choices should be explicit and task-scaled
cards.summary: For FEM+IPC soft-body tasks, pick explicit Elastic material parameters and contact friction from the task and deformable config bounds instead of relying on hidden defaults.
guidance: Generated FEM elastic materials should pass explicit E, nu, rho, and friction_mu values.
Choose E, nu, and rho within deformable_cfg bounds; use defaults unless the task calls for a special material.
Use E around 1e4-5e4 for very soft gel, 5e4-5e5 for soft rubber, and 5e5-5e6 for firmer elastomer-like bodies.
Use nu around 0.2 for compressible foam-like objects, 0.35 as a balanced default, and 0.45 only when nearly incompressible behavior is needed.
Use rho around 300 for light foam-like material, 1000 as a water-like gel/rubber default, and 3000 for a heavy dense soft solid.
Choose friction_mu from the material/contact role; do not read a nonexistent fem_friction_mu from deformable_cfg.

Use friction_mu around 0.0-0.1 for slippery contact, 0.2-0.5 for typical soft rubber/plastic contact, and 0.6-1.0 for high-friction sticky contact unless the task clearly justifies an out-of-range value.
The same FEM material should also pass model, hydroelastic_modulus, contact_resistance, and hessian_invariant from deformable_cfg when the generated worker creates FEM Elastic materials.
dispatch_hints: Send to body when a generated FEM entity or material is being written or repaired.
Send to critic when checking whether a soft-body result is genuinely FEM rather than rigid-only.
Send to opt when material stiffness, density, damping, or friction are plausible continuous tuning variables.

cards.title: Soft-body scenes should use robust chunky geometry, positive clearances, and observable deformation
cards.summary: FEM+IPC soft-body layouts should avoid thin fragile geometry and precise interlocks unless required, using conservative spacing, broad supports, and visible deformation metrics.
guidance: Prefer chunky volumetric soft bodies with dimensions large enough for stable tet discretization and visible deformation.
