Use broad support surfaces and positive gaps rather than precise initial interlock or nearly coincident contact.
For stacked, bridge, column, or leveling tasks, make initial placements tolerant: centers, heights, and support widths should leave clear physical margins.
Choose material stiffness and friction so deformation is visible but not numerically explosive.
Record deformation proxies such as height change, bbox spread, COM, contact/support events, and final stability.
For moving rigid tools pressing soft bodies, use staged approach and bounded force/gain rather than sudden penetration.
restrictions: Do not use cloth/rope/thin-shell style geometry for volumetric FEM tasks unless the task explicitly demands it and the pipeline supports FEM.Cloth for that case.
Do not rely on exact mesh interlock, tiny clearances, or coincident surfaces for success.
Do not fake soft-body behavior with rigid substitutes or post-step state writes.
checks: Check that tet_resolution, object size, and material stiffness are mutually plausible.
Check that initial soft bodies have positive clearance from supports, tools, and each other.
Check that rendered evidence and metrics show deformation rather than only rigid translation.
dispatch_hints: Send to Planner for deformable scene planning.
Send to scene/body for FEM geometry, material, and initial layout.
Send to action when a moving tool compresses or manipulates soft bodies.
Send to critic and opt when evaluating deformation quality or tuning material/contact/layout variables.

provenance.motivation: Add robust layout heuristics for current deformable column/block/bridge-style suites.

cards.title: Initial IPC world failures usually point to geometry, spacing, or asset validity
cards.summary: When libuipc reports invalid initial geometry before a rigid ABD accessor exception, route the repair to placement, scale, clearance, orientation, or generated asset topology before blaming IPC capability.
guidance: Treat World-is-not-valid and SimplicialSurfaceIntersectionCheck/DistanceCheck diagnostics as primary evidence of invalid initial IPC geometry.
Repair the source that controls initial placement, scale, spacing, orientation, duplicate IPC geometry, mesh clearance, or asset topology.
Preserve the intended IPC contact/coupling model unless a clean no-penetration repro still proves capability is missing.
Do not mask the issue by disabling IPC, disabling coupling, changing to hidden constraints, or bypassing contact.
dispatch_hints: Send to scene/body for placement, spacing, orientation, and coupling mode repairs.
Send to planner when generated mesh/XML assets appear topologically unsuitable and should be regenerated.
Send to critic when classifying IPC execution logs and avoiding secondary-accessor misdiagnosis.

cards.title: World-is-not-valid IPC logs should trigger geometry-first diagnosis
cards.summary: When execution logs show IPC world validity, intersection, distance, barrier, or thickness errors, Planner and Critic should classify the failure as a geometry/asset/clearance problem before treating later errors as primary.
guidance: Treat the first IPC world-validity or intersection/distance diagnostic as the primary failure, even if later logs mention rigid ABD accessors or state retrieval.
Check initial object clearance, duplicate support surfaces, mesh scale, XML collision geometry, rotated primitive extents, and generated asset topology.
If ready generated assets may be involved, use inspect_assets before repeatedly asking body/action to repair choreography.
If inspection shows intrinsic topology, filled holes, wrong scale, inverted volume, or unsuitable XML morphology, route to Planner asset regeneration.
If assets are usable, route to scene/body for placement, orientation, spacing, support surface, or coupling-mode repair.
restrictions: Do not fix this failure by disabling IPC, disabling contact, hiding collision, switching to rigid-only behavior, or adding hidden supports.
Do not treat secondary accessor errors as the root cause unless reproduced without any initial-geometry diagnostic.
checks: Check stderr/stdout ordering to identify the first primary diagnostic.
Check whether the same contact region has duplicate planes or coincident supports.
Check whether any generated mesh/XML asset has suspicious bbox, scale, topology, or collision geometry.
dispatch_hints: Send to critic when classifying execution failures.
Send to scene/body when repairing initial layout or support geometry.
Send to planner when asset inspection or regeneration may be needed.

provenance.motivation: Make common IPC execution-log signatures actionable and source-routed.

cards.title: Route repairs to the source owner with concrete evidence
cards.summary: Repair advice should name the owner module or Planner asset action, the wrong behavior, the evidence, the likely source cause, and what a convincing fix must change.
guidance: Compare the original prompt, generated source, execution reports, metrics, event logs, visual output, and module ownership.
Name the owner module, concrete wrong behavior, evidence proving it, likely source-level cause, and expected fix.
Avoid vague advice like "improve the trajectory" when concrete evidence exists, and do not compress important source-level feedback just to keep the answer short.
Route mesh-intrinsic defects to Planner asset regeneration rather than asking body/scene/action/rendering to patch geometry.
Mesh-intrinsic defects include failed manifold checks, Genesis FEM import validation failure, missing/corrupt texture, wrong generated topology, and visual/runtime mesh pairing defects.
Route XML/MJCF mechanism defects to Planner XML regeneration rather than repeatedly repairing action timing.

XML/MJCF mechanism defects include missing opposing thumb/fingers, missing useful actuator affordances, wrong joint axes, invalid link hierarchy, and body geometry that cannot perform the requested contact task.
dispatch_hints: Send to critic for failure classification.
Send to planner when deciding request_repair versus start_mesh_assets/start_xml_assets.
Send to body or action repair workers when the latest evidence points to source-level body construction, asset use, mechanism structure, controller timing, or contact semantics.
Send to opt when Opt returns needs_rewrite and must give owner-specific guidance.

cards.title: Frame dynamic subjects so they fill most of the render
cards.summary: Rendering should choose camera target, distance, and FOV from the main dynamic objects across the episode so the requested behavior is large and centered enough to inspect.
guidance: When generating or repairing rendering, estimate the main dynamic objects' initial, mid-run, and final bounding boxes, then choose camera target, distance, and FOV so the main behavior occupies about 55%-80% of the image.
Do not let the main subjects shrink into a small corner or a tiny central region.
For objects with large travel, prefer a better camera center, elevated oblique view, and moderate distance over blindly looking at the world origin.
Use a slightly wider view only when the action would otherwise leave the frame, and keep the behavior readable rather than merely visible.
restrictions: Do not accept a physically correct render if the requested contact, deformation, motion, or interaction cannot be judged because the subjects are too small.
Do not optimize camera framing by cropping away necessary start, impact, or final-settle context.
checks: Inspect sampled frames or contact sheets for subject fill ratio, centering, and whether the main action remains in frame.
Check render_stats.json records camera position, lookat, FOV, and resolution when available.
dispatch_hints: Send to rendering for camera setup and repair.
Send to critic when a result is semantically plausible but visually too small or off-center.
Send to opt only if camera variables are explicitly part of a visual-readability repair.

provenance.motivation: Make rendered evidence fill the frame enough for human review.

cards.title: Critic evidence must come from the latest completed execution
cards.summary: Planner and Critic should reject stale metrics, videos, render stats, and summaries left from earlier runs when the latest execution failed, timed out, or produced only partial artifacts.
guidance: Treat execution_report.json as the source of truth for the latest run status, start time, exit code, timeout flag, and workspace directory.
Use metrics.json, event_log.json, run_result.json, summary.json, render_stats.json, frames, and render.mp4 only if they were produced by the latest completed execution.
Before rerunning a failed or repaired case, clean or clearly version output artifacts so Critic cannot confuse stale successful artifacts with current failed execution.
When the latest execution times out or exits nonzero, require a clean successful rerun before accepting task success even if old video or metrics files still exist.
If artifacts are partial, report the missing current evidence and route repair to execution/integration/rendering/action/body according to the current failure rather than judging stale behavior.
restrictions: Do not pass an episode using metrics, render_stats, frames, or video whose timestamps or run identifiers predate the latest execution_report started_at time.
Do not use stale completed video to mask a later timeout, nonzero exit, or source repair that never finished.
Do not let a success flag in an old summary override a current execution failure.
checks: Check execution_report.status, exit_code, timed_out, started_at_unix, and duration_sec before inspecting semantic success.
Check artifact timestamps, run identifiers, frame counts, render_stats frame totals, and video presence against the latest execution.
Check whether current frames cover the requested duration and whether render.mp4 exists for the latest run.
Check whether summary/run_result success agrees with the latest metrics and execution status.
dispatch_hints: Send to critic whenever execution failed or timed out but metrics/video artifacts are still present.
Send to planner when a repair should rerun from a clean artifact directory or invalidate stale evidence.
Send to rendering when video/render_stats/frame evidence is incomplete or inconsistent after execution.

provenance.motivation: Several cases retained old metrics or videos after later failed executions, making Critic evidence inconsistent with the latest run.

cards.title: Use a subtly tinted ground on white backgrounds
cards.summary: When the background is pure white and a ground plane is present, use a light non-white ground color unless the prompt explicitly requests white ground.
guidance: If the render uses a pure white background and includes a ground/floor, do not make the ground pure white unless the prompt explicitly requires white ground.
Use a low-saturation light color such as light gray, pale blue-gray, or pale warm gray so the horizon, contact shadows, and support surface remain visible.
Keep the ground subtle enough that it does not distract from task objects, but distinct enough to separate from the sky/background.
restrictions: Do not override a prompt that explicitly asks for a white floor or a specific ground material.
Do not use dark or saturated floors when the task only needs a neutral evidence render.
checks: Inspect sampled frames for a visible floor boundary, contact shadow, or surface cue.
Check scene/rendering code for a non-white floor material when background_color is white.
dispatch_hints: Send to scene/rendering when floor material or background color is chosen.
Send to critic when objects appear to float or the ground plane is hard to distinguish.

provenance.motivation: Avoid invisible pure-white floors on white evidence backgrounds.

cards.title: Visual evidence must be readable enough to judge the requested behavior
cards.summary: Generated videos and contact sheets should clearly show task-relevant objects, contacts, and motion; unclear rendering should be repaired rather than accepted.
guidance: When the task does not specify a background or environment, prefer a plain pure-white background for ordinary evidence renders so objects, contacts, and motion stay easy to inspect.

For Genesis Rasterizer camera renders, set the default light background in scene.py when constructing gs.Scene: pass vis_options=gs.options.VisOptions(background_color=(1.0, 1.0, 1.0), ambient_light=(0.55, 0.55, 0.55), lights=(...)) or a similarly bright near-white background.

Do not rely on scene.add_light to create the white background. scene.add_light only adds explicit lights for supported renderers and does not replace VisOptions.background_color; with the default Rasterizer it may be unsupported.

If the case needs non-white environmental context, document that choice in the plan and render_stats.json; otherwise scene.py should explicitly set VisOptions.background_color rather than inheriting the Genesis dark default.
restrictions: Do not treat unclear rendering as acceptable when the simulation behavior cannot be judged.
Do not let opt report success or partial success from numeric score alone when visual evidence is missing, unreadable, or contradicts the goal.
Camera position, lookat, field of view, clipping, lighting, background, capture cadence, and resolution should make the task behavior inspectable.
When sampled frame paths, contact sheets, texture summaries, or texture-presence warnings are available, use them as review evidence alongside numeric metrics instead of relying only on event logs.
checks: Inspect sampled frames or video before accepting success.
When no non-white environment is requested, check generated scene.py for gs.options.VisOptions with an explicit bright background_color and ambient_light passed into gs.Scene.
Record final camera and rendering choices in render_stats.json when the generated case supports it.
If meshes or textures are involved, check whether orientation, scale, material binding, and rendered texture appearance are consistent with the source and manifest.
dispatch_hints: Send to scene when the background color or ambient lighting must be set through gs.Scene vis_options.
Send to rendering when evidence is hard to inspect because of view, lighting, cropping, or capture choices.
Send to critic and opt when deciding whether numeric success is trustworthy.

cards.title: Rigid and contact tasks need observable contact, pose, and support metrics
cards.summary: Rigid, articulated, and rigid-IPC tasks should record task-relevant physical metrics so Critic and Opt can distinguish real contact success from visual coincidence.
guidance: For rigid/contact tasks, metrics should include task object pose, target distance, height, velocity, contact counts, and relevant relative distances.
For support, stacking, catching, containment, or grasp tasks, record whether the object is supported, contained, or grasped using geometry- or contact-based evidence rather than only final position.
For articulated mechanisms, record useful joint/DOF state, actuator command summaries, and object-relative end-effector/tool distances.
For rolling, sliding, or pushing tasks, record displacement, direction error, speed, and whether motion happened through contact with the intended driver.
Metrics should expose shaped residuals that Opt can use when the case is close but not passing.
restrictions: Do not treat final visual overlap or final target proximity as sufficient evidence when contact, support, or containment is the requested behavior.
Do not create metrics by copying target values or expected success labels into runtime output.
checks: Check that metrics.json contains both final outcome metrics and at least one intermediate/event metric for the main interaction.
Check that Critic can infer why the object moved, not only where it ended.
dispatch_hints: Send to body/action when implementing metrics or event logs for rigid/contact tasks.
Send to critic when a result appears visually plausible but physical contact evidence is weak.
Send to opt when continuous residuals require measurable objective terms.

provenance.motivation: Add runtime evidence guidance for rigid/contact tasks beyond migrated prompt text.

cards.title: Use readable, appealing visual styling for simulation evidence
cards.summary: Planner, asset decisions, scene/body/rendering workers, and Critic should treat visual readability and tasteful object styling as soft quality goals alongside semantics and physics.
guidance: Planner, asset decisions, scene/body/rendering workers, and Critic should inspect not only semantic and physics correctness, but also whether the video is visually readable and easy to review.
Treat subject size, ground/background contrast, viewing angle, occlusion, color separation, lighting, and one-glance motion legibility as soft quality goals.

Visual readability is not normally a hard pass/fail condition by itself, but repair advice and generated visual choices should push the result toward clearer, more polished, and easier-to-review output when semantics are otherwise close.

When the prompt does not specify object colors, avoid defaulting all important objects to gray. Assign distinct, role-aware colors so different actors, obstacles, targets, supports, and actuators are easy to distinguish.

Prefer soft, low-to-medium saturation colors with clear value contrast: muted teal, soft blue, gentle green, warm orange, dusty rose, lavender, pale yellow, or warm clay. Use gray mainly for neutral supports, floors, metal fixtures, or intentionally background objects.

Use a clean character-animation visualization style: simple readable shapes, uncluttered backgrounds, subtle floor cues, soft shadows, and a small number of coordinated colors that clarify motion rather than distract from it.

Use color semantically: the main dynamic actor gets the strongest readable color; passive obstacles and supports get quieter colors; goals, handles, or actuators may receive a consistent accent color.
Keep color contrast high enough for separation, but avoid high-saturation neon colors unless the prompt explicitly requests them.
restrictions: Do not let numerical metrics alone override visibly confusing, tiny, occluded, or low-contrast evidence.
Do not recommend purely cosmetic changes that would break physical semantics or hide failure evidence.
Do not leave multiple important prompt-unspecified objects as the same gray material when color separation would make the behavior easier to inspect.
Do not use highly saturated, neon, or rainbow palettes as the default style; reserve them for prompts that explicitly ask for bright, toy-like, or stylized colors.
Do not choose colors that make contact, deformation, or relative motion harder to see against the ground/background.
checks: Review sampled frames and video, not only metrics and event logs.
When proposing repair, separate hard correctness failures from soft visual-readability improvements.
Check whether the main actor, passive objects, obstacles, supports, and targets are visually separable in sampled frames.
Check whether unspecified colors were chosen intentionally rather than falling back to gray/default materials.
Check that the palette is coordinated and low-to-medium saturation unless the prompt asked for vivid colors.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when visual styling, asset requests, or worker dispatch may affect readability.
Send to scene/body/rendering when choosing materials, colors, asset appearance, camera, lighting, or visual evidence setup.
Send to critic by default when render artifacts exist.
Send to opt only when visual-readability variables are intentionally being tuned.

provenance.motivation: Encourage Planner, visual-generation workers, and Critic to improve readability and review quality without turning aesthetics into a hard gate.

cards.title: Optimized variables must affect runtime behavior and be measured
cards.summary: Opt should expose only compact, physically meaningful continuous variables whose sampled values survive generated-code clamps and are reported as effective runtime values.
restrictions: Do not expose rendering, camera, lighting, visual-only, irrelevant, or immediately overwritten constants as opt variables.
Keep default params and bounds inside the post-clamp effective range used by generated code.

If generated action/body code has version gates, sign-sensitive schedule guards, controller floors, clamps, or XML/joint range limits, encode those invariants in default_opt_params.json and opt_space.json.
Include required version fields in default params, omit variables that will be ignored, and keep defaults/bounds inside the effective range after generated-code clamps.
Before reporting success, compare requested opt params against metrics-reported effective controls, material values, or layout values.
If active variables are ignored, clamped to constants, or missing from metrics, repair hooks/contracts and rerun before calling the result useful.
checks: Run at least one baseline and one perturbed rollout when optimization is appropriate.
Verify metrics include effective values, not only requested values or final score.
dispatch_hints: Send to opt whenever Planner asks for continuous tuning or when critic evidence suggests a bounded scalar search.
Send to critic when reviewing whether an optimization result is real progress rather than a no-op search.

cards.title: Opt metrics and objectives must be observable, shaped, and task-faithful
cards.summary: Optimization should expose measurable simulation residuals and explicit success criteria instead of optimizing target copies, binary-only pass flags, or custom target transforms.
guidance: If Opt adds metrics, make them observable quantities from simulation state, not values copied from the target.
Define shaped objective terms whenever possible: distance-to-target, velocity error, event timing, contact count, closest approach, pose/joint error, stability margin, or task-object retention.
Keep binary pass/fail as success_criteria, not as the only optimization signal.
Always define explicit success_criteria. Score improvement alone is not task success.
Do not use transform="custom" in target_spec.json. If a custom score is needed, compute that scalar inside generated case metrics and reference it with transform="identity".
dispatch_hints: Send to Opt when target_spec.json or metrics need to be created or repaired.
Send to Critic when checking whether an Opt result is real task success rather than numeric score movement.

cards.title: Route to Opt only for runnable cases with bounded continuous residuals
cards.summary: Planner should use Opt for close, physically plausible failures that can be improved by compact continuous parameters, and route structural failures back to source repair or asset regeneration.
guidance: Good Opt candidates are runnable cases with plausible entities, readable evidence, and continuous measurable misses.
Continuous residuals may involve timing, target distance, pose, speed, angle, friction, damping, gain, initial layout, material/contact, XML scalar, or solver/contact tolerance.

Bad Opt candidates are structural failures: missing entities, invalid assets, broken imports, impossible geometry, stuck mechanisms, absent metrics, invisible behavior, or task semantics that need rewriting.
After useful Opt results, rerun execution so final artifacts reflect the selected params.
dispatch_hints: Send to planner when deciding run_opt versus request_repair/regeneration.
Send to opt with the effective-parameter restriction when optimization is selected.
Send to critic when deciding whether a failure is structural or parameter-limited.

cards.title: Opt search spaces should be compact, meaningful, and runner-native
cards.summary: Opt should tune a small set of physically meaningful scalar variables, use log scaling for positive variables spanning orders of magnitude, and rely on the generic runner strategy knobs.
guidance: Keep the search space small. Prefer 2-8 variables unless evidence justifies more.
Prefer scale="log" for positive variables spanning orders of magnitude, such as stiffness, damping, density, contact stiffness, tolerances, and controller gains.
Use the runner's generic strategy knobs when useful rather than hand-coding task-specific search logic.
strategy.phases can optimize one variable group at a time, strategy.restarts can run multiple seeds/sigma scales, and strategy.early_stop can stop after success or several non-improving generations.
Do not use low-fidelity timing/render shortcuts unless Planner explicitly asks for them.
dispatch_hints: Send to Opt before creating opt_space.json or tuning CMA-ES strategy.
Send to Planner when deciding whether a failure is close enough for bounded continuous search.

cards.title: Opt must preserve the task semantics and target
cards.summary: Optimization may tune physical parameters and controls, but it must not make the task easier by changing required entities, target semantics, or the requested success condition.
restrictions: Do not change the task semantics, required entities, or success target to make the case easier.
Do not move the goal to the object or relax the requested target.
Initial-setting and layout variables may only tune the setup within the prompt's intended scene family.

Optimization must tune honest physical parameters or controls that the generated simulation uses, not hidden constraints, fake attachments, direct post-initialization object state writes, or task-object teleportation.
dispatch_hints: Send to Opt whenever initial layout, goal placement, or success criteria are considered as variables.
Send to Critic when reviewing whether an optimized success still matches the original text prompt.

cards.title: Opt may only patch safe XML scalar attributes
cards.summary: When Opt touches XML/MJCF assets, it must preserve topology and only change physically meaningful numeric attributes on existing named elements.
restrictions: Prefer runtime hooks for XML actuator tuning when Genesis APIs can express them, such as set_dofs_kp, set_dofs_kv, set_dofs_force_range, or equivalent control APIs.
Use direct XML edits only for scalar attributes that cannot be set reliably at runtime.
If editing or generating per-run copies of assets/xml/**/*.xml, keep the source asset topology unchanged.

Only numeric attributes on existing named elements may change: actuator kp, forcerange, ctrlrange; joint damping, armature, range; and physically meaningful geom/contact scalars such as friction, solref, solimp, density, and mass.
Do not add, remove, or rename bodies, joints, geoms, actuators, meshes, defaults, or change joint axes.
Validate the XML diff yourself and include evidence beginning with xml_scalar_patch_validated= when reporting a useful XML-touching result.
dispatch_hints: Send to Opt whenever XML scalar attributes are exposed as optimization variables.
Send to Critic when checking whether an Opt result changed XML morphology rather than scalar parameters.

cards.title: Planner should dispatch focused SimDebug cards to each downstream role
cards.summary: Planner should use the simdebug_cards action field to send every card that is relevant to the current evidence and declared role scope, while avoiding unrelated default card noise.
guidance: Use simdebug_cards when the current action has clear task- or evidence-specific needs; leave it null only when all role/physics-compatible candidates are useful.
Before placing a card id under simdebug_cards.<role>, check the candidate card's declared scopes and only assign it to that role when scopes includes that role or all.

If a card is conceptually useful but its declared scopes do not include the target role, route the evidence through a scoped owner such as planner or critic, or update the card library before relying on it.
For generated mesh use, dispatch generated_mesh_manifest_usage_guideline, planner_asset_retry_guideline, scale_policy_restriction, and render_visual_evidence_restriction as relevant.

For XML/MJCF mechanisms, dispatch xml_asset_request_contract_guideline, external_articulation_mjcf_guideline, collision_contact_restriction, physical_causality_restriction, and controller_schedule_guideline, especially when the asset may contain dummy mount/base geoms, collision masks, grippers, rollers, hinges, or other contact tools.

For FEM+IPC soft bodies, dispatch deformable_fem_ipc_scope_restriction, ipc_fem_material_selection_guideline, fem_ipc_initial_geometry_restriction, fem_state_metrics_guideline, and soft_body_robust_layout_guideline.

For Opt, dispatch opt_routing_guideline, opt_effective_parameter_restriction, opt_metric_and_objective_design_guideline, opt_search_space_design_guideline, and any physics-specific metric/material cards.
Update dispatched cards after new evidence appears; repair, critic, and opt calls should not blindly reuse cards chosen before execution.
checks: Before spawn_workers, check whether each role receives cards matching its actual ownership and each selected card's declared scope.
Before request_repair, check whether the selected cards reflect the latest failure evidence.
Before request_repair, check that every simdebug_cards.<role> entry is scoped to that role; Python will filter incompatible cards, but Planner should avoid sending them.
Before run_opt, check that the selected cards distinguish parameter-limited failures from structural failures.
